content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
In {{STATE_NAME}}, is it smarter to rent and invest the difference,
or buy a home and build equity?

slides.speakerNotes: Read Amy's scenarioŌĆöemphasize the competing factors she faces. Ask: "What would YOU do in Amy's situation? Why?" Poll class for initial reactions, then revisit at end of lesson.

objectives.description: Analyze the financial implications of renting versus owning a home in {{STATE_NAME}}, including upfront costs, monthly expenses, and long-term wealth building.
Recognize key factors that influence housing decisions including financial readiness, location, lifestyle preferences, and future goals.
Assess how personal circumstancesŌĆöcareer stability, family plans, and financial positionŌĆöaffect which housing choice is optimal.
Apply critical thinking to housing scenarios and calculate the long-term financial impact using {{STATE_NAME}} housing costs.

terms.definition: A legal agreement outlining terms under which one party agrees to rent property from another, typically for 6-12 months.
terms.example: A 12-month lease in {{STATE_NAME}} specifying ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}}/month rent, pet policies, and maintenance responsibilities

terms.definition: The difference between a property's current market value and the amount still owed on the mortgageŌĆöyour ownership stake.
terms.example: Home worth ${{STATE_MEDIAN_HOME_PRICE}} with $350K mortgage = significant equity (your net worth in the property)

terms.definition: A loan used to purchase a home where the property itself serves as collateral, typically repaid over 15-30 years.
terms.example: A 30-year fixed mortgage at {{STATE_AVG_MORTGAGE_RATE}}% means consistent monthly payments for 360 months

terms.definition: An annual tax paid by homeowners based on the assessed value of their property, used to fund local services.
terms.example: In {{STATE_NAME}}, property tax averages {{STATE_PROPERTY_TAX_AVG}}% of home value annually

leftPanel.title: Financial Aspects of Renting
paragraphs: Renting involves paying a property owner for the right to live in their property for a specific period governed by a lease agreement.
Monthly costs include: Rent payment, renter's insurance ($15-30/mo), utilities, and sometimes parking.
Upfront costs: Security deposit (1-2 months' rent) and first month's rentŌĆötypically $3,000-$5,000 total in {{STATE_NAME}}.

highlightBox.text: Ē▓Ī Renters are NOT responsible for major repairs, property taxes, or maintenanceŌĆöthe landlord covers these costs.

leftColumn.title: Ē┐ó Renting
items: Flexibility to move when lease ends

leftPanel.title: Financial Aspects of Owning
paragraphs: Owning means purchasing property with a mortgage loan, giving you full rights as long as you maintain payments.
Monthly costs include: Mortgage (principal + interest), property taxes, homeowner's insurance, HOA fees (if applicable), and maintenance.
Upfront costs: Down payment (3-20% of ${{STATE_MEDIAN_HOME_PRICE}}) plus closing costs (2-5%).

highlightBox.text: Ē▓░ Each mortgage payment builds equityŌĆöyour ownership stake grows over time as you pay down the loan.

layoutData.title: What Should Guide Your Housing Choice?
paragraphs: The rent vs. buy decision is deeply personal and depends on multiple interconnected factors. There's no universally "right" answerŌĆöonly what's right for your specific situation in {{STATE_NAME}}.
bulletPoints: Financial Readiness: Savings for down payment, credit score, stable income, emergency fund
Time Horizon: How long you plan to stay (generally need 5+ years to make buying worthwhile)
Career Stability: Job security, potential relocations, remote work flexibility
Local Market: {{STATE_NAME}}'s price-to-rent ratio, appreciation trends, market conditions
Lifestyle Preferences: Desire to customize, willingness to handle maintenance
Family Plans: Growing family may need more space and stability

content.headerTitle: Why This Matters for Your Future

layoutData.title: Housing = Your Largest Expense
paragraphs: Housing is typically the largest expense you'll face in your lifetime. Whether you rent or buy in {{STATE_NAME}}, this decision significantly impacts your financial situation, lifestyle, and long-term wealth building potential.
bulletPoints: Monthly Budget: Housing should ideally be under 28-30% of gross income
Wealth Building: Homeowners have 40x higher net worth than renters (median)
Retirement Security: Owning a paid-off home reduces retirement expenses dramatically
Flexibility vs. Stability: Your life stage determines which matters more
Opportunity Cost: Money spent on housing can't be invested elsewhere
{{STATE_NAME}} Market: Local conditions affect which option makes more sense

slides.speakerNotes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS:
ŌĆó ""Renting is throwing money away."" ŌåÆ Rent provides housing, flexibility, and no maintenance responsibility. Homeownership has costs that don't build equity either (interest, taxes, insurance, maintenance).
ŌĆó ""Buying is always a good investment."" ŌåÆ Homes can lose value; transaction costs are high; and if you move within 5-7 years, you may not recoup your costs. Location and timing matter significantly.
ŌĆó ""I need 20% down to buy a home."" ŌåÆ Many loans allow 3-10% down, but lower down payments mean PMI (private mortgage insurance) and higher monthly costs. 20% is optimal but not required.

leftPanel.title: Current Housing Reality
paragraphs: Today's housing market presents unique challenges for young adults. Rising prices, higher interest rates, and limited inventory have made homeownership increasingly difficult.
Many successful financial strategies now include renting while investing the differenceŌĆösometimes outperforming homeownership in high-cost markets.
The key is making an informed decision based on your specific circumstances in {{STATE_NAME}}, not following outdated rules or social pressure.

highlightBox.text: Ē┤ä Housing needs change over time. The right choice today might be different five years from now.

scenario.name: Amy's Smart Renting Decision
paragraphs: Amy is a 25-year-old software developer who recently started her career in {{STATE_NAME}}. She chose to rent an apartment near downtown for ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}}/month, close to her workplace and entertainment options.

Her reasoning: As a young professional, Amy values flexibility to accept job opportunities in different locations. Instead of saving for a down payment, she invests the difference in her 401(k) and index funds.
Monthly breakdown: ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}} rent + $20 renter's insurance + $150 utilities = approximately ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}} + $170/month total

scenario.name: John & Maria's Homeownership
paragraphs: John and Maria, a married couple in their 30s with stable careers in {{STATE_NAME}}, purchased a home at the median price of ${{STATE_MEDIAN_HOME_PRICE}} with a 10% down payment and closing costs.

Their reasoning: With established careers and plans to start a family, they wanted stability and the ability to build equity. A fixed-rate mortgage at {{STATE_AVG_MORTGAGE_RATE}}% gives them predictable payments.
Monthly breakdown: ${{CALCULATED_MONTHLY_MORTGAGE}} mortgage + ${{CALCULATED_PROPERTY_TAX_MONTHLY}} taxes + $150 insurance + $200 maintenance = substantial monthly investment

content.question:
Given {{STATE_NAME}}'s median home price of ${{STATE_MEDIAN_HOME_PRICE}} and median rent of ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}}, which option makes more sense for someone in their 20s?

What factors would change your answer?

What are your top priorities when thinking about where to live in {{STATE_NAME}}?

How might your housing preferences change over the next 5-10 years as your life circumstances evolve?

questions.question: What is equity, and why is building equity considered an advantage of homeownership?
In {{STATE_NAME}}, with a {{STATE_PROPERTY_TAX_AVG}}% property tax rate, how much would property taxes add to monthly costs on a ${{STATE_MEDIAN_HOME_PRICE}} home?
Why might renting at ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}}/month be the smarter financial choice for someone early in their career?
What hidden costs of homeownership are renters NOT responsible for paying?

slides.speakerNotes:
ASSESSMENT ANSWER KEY:
1. C (5-7 years or more) ŌĆö This timeframe allows buyers to recoup closing costs, build equity, and potentially benefit from appreciation.
2. The Martinez family has: (1) stable careers with no relocation risk, (2) a 10+ year commitment allowing equity building and cost recovery, (3) sufficient savings for down payment plus emergency fund, (4) the need for school district stability.
3. Amy should prioritize RENTING because: (1) potential relocation in 2-3 years doesn't allow time to recover buying costs, (2) her savings would be depleted by a down payment, leaving no emergency fund.
4. Equity is the difference between your home's value and what you owe. As you pay down your mortgage, you own more of your home. It's like "forced savings" and can be accessed through sale or home equity loans.
5. Responses should reflect thoughtful consideration of career stability, family plans, location preferences, and financial readiness. Look for recognition that housing needs change with life circumstances.

takeaways.title: No Universal "Right" Answer
takeaways.description: The best housing choice depends on your personal financial situation, career, and life goals

takeaways.title: Renting Has Advantages
takeaways.description: Flexibility, lower upfront costs, no maintenanceŌĆörenting is NOT "throwing money away"

takeaways.title: Buying Builds Wealth
takeaways.description: Homeownership offers equity building, stability, and potential appreciation over time

takeaways.title: Know Your Numbers
takeaways.description: Compare ${{STATE_MEDIAN_RENT}} rent vs. ${{CALCULATED_MONTHLY_MORTGAGE}}+ mortgage to make informed decisions

steps: Estimate equity built after 5 years of ownership
